data.title: Light-tier reviewer (haiku) produces false positives on plugin config files — prefer standard tier for config reviews
data.description:
## What happened

In a host project, the light-tier code reviewer (haiku) flagged required plugin config keys as issues — a false positive. The haiku model lacks sufficient context about the DSO plugin's config schema to distinguish required keys from unnecessary ones.

## Root cause

The light-tier reviewer (dso:code-reviewer-light, haiku) has limited context capacity and does not have access to the full config schema documentation. When reviewing changes to config files (dso-config.conf, settings.json, workflow-config.yaml), it cannot distinguish between required plugin keys and unnecessary additions, leading to false-positive findings.

## Expected behavior

Config file changes should receive at minimum a standard-tier review (sonnet), which has enough context to understand the plugin's config schema and distinguish required keys from extraneous ones.

## Fix needed

1. Add config file patterns to the review-complexity-classifier.sh floor rules so that changes to config files (dso-config.conf, settings.json, workflow-config.yaml) receive a minimum score that routes to standard tier instead of light tier
2. Alternatively, add a file-type override in the classifier: if the diff touches config files matching known plugin config patterns, set a floor of score=3 (standard tier minimum)
3. Document this as a known limitation of light-tier review: haiku lacks domain knowledge for config schema validation
